title: Chocolate tempering
subtitle: Seeding method for a glossy, snap-set chocolate finish
excerpt: The seeding method for tempering dark chocolate: melting two-thirds of the chocolate, then stirring in reserved chopped chocolate to bring the temperature to the working range. Produces a glossy, firm-set result without specialist equipment.
sourceNotes: Chocolate tempering is a professional confectionery technique documented in French patisserie from the nineteenth century. The science of cocoa butter crystal formation that underpins tempering was formalised in the twentieth century. The seeding method (adding unmelted chocolate to melted chocolate to introduce stable crystals) is a domestic-accessible alternative to the tabling method and produces comparable results. The target temperatures for dark chocolate are stable across all professional sources.

content.text: controls which crystal form the cocoa butter sets in. The seeding method uses reserved unmelted chocolate as a source of stable Form V crystals, stirring them into fully melted chocolate to bring it back into the working temperature range without specialist equipment. For dark chocolate: melt to 50-55°C, seed down to 28-29°C, bring back to 31-32°C. Work immediately at 31-32°C.

Use a digital instant-read thermometer, not a candy thermometer. The temperature windows are narrow (2-3°C) and a slow-responding thermometer will cause you to over- or under-shoot. Test the temper before committing to the full batch: dip a small piece of baking paper into the chocolate and set it on the bench. It should set firm and glossy within 3-5 minutes at room temperature.

attrs.prepNote: 60-70% cocoa solids, finely chopped, divided: 300 g to melt, 100 g reserved for seeding

content.text: Put 300 g of the {{dark-chocolate}} in a heatproof bowl over a pan of barely simmering water. The bowl must not touch the water and no steam should escape into the chocolate. Stir gently until melted and the temperature reads 50-55°C. Remove from the heat.

Add the reserved 100 g of finely chopped chocolate in three additions, stirring constantly after each addition until fully melted and incorporated. Check the temperature between additions. The chocolate should drop toward the target range of 28-29°C as the cold chocolate seeds it. If it reaches 28°C before all the chocolate is added, stop adding and stir until the temperature stabilises.

Briefly return the bowl to the pan of hot water (removed from the heat) for 5-10 seconds, stirring constantly, until the temperature rises to 31-32°C: the working temperature for dark chocolate. Test the temper on baking paper. If it passes, use the chocolate immediately for dipping, moulding, or spreading. If the chocolate cools below 29°C during work, briefly warm it back to 31-32°C over the hot water.
